Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Consciousness Science

Victor Lamme

b. 1959

Recurrent processing as the neural basis of phenomenal experience.

Victor Lamme’s position is best understood as a neuro-revisionary, first-order, recurrent-processing theory of phenomenal consciousness. Its guiding claim is not merely that recurrent neural activity correlates with consciousness, but that the scientific concept of visual consciousness should be rebuilt around recurrent processing itself. On this view, consciousness is not fundamentally report, introspective certainty, attention, working memory, language, higher-order thought, or global availability for action. Those are later or additional cognitive functions. The core phenomenon—seeing, in the phenomenal sense—is produced when sensory representations enter sufficiently rich recurrent or re-entrant interactions, especially within the visual system. Lamme’s slogan-level contrast is: consciousness is seeing; access is knowing that one sees. His mature view is therefore one of the sharpest neuroscientific defenses of phenomenal consciousness “overflowing” cognitive access.

There is an important scope limitation. Lamme’s own work is overwhelmingly about visual consciousness. In his 2018 paper on the “seeing or knowing” debate and panpsychism, he explicitly says that the paper focuses almost entirely on visual consciousness, not because other modalities or executive faculties are irrelevant, but because of his own limits regarding those other domains. A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Lamme is therefore a reconstruction: the visual theory is the evidential core, and broader conclusions about mind, animal consciousness, artificial consciousness, selfhood, thought, emotion, and agency must be treated as extrapolations from that core.

1. Historical position: where Lamme stands in the philosophy of mind

Historically, Lamme belongs to the empirical turn in consciousness studies that took shape after the 1990s, when neuroscience, psychology, biology, computation, and philosophy increasingly converged on the problem of consciousness. Lamme himself frames the post-1990s field as one in which multiple scientific theories arose, backed by experimental data, but where disagreement remained over the basic explanandum: is consciousness the fact that we experience the world, or the fact that we can reflect on, access, and report what we experience?

Within contemporary taxonomy, Lamme’s theory is standardly classified as Recurrent Processing Theory. It is one of the major competitors to Global Neuronal Workspace Theory, Higher-Order Theories, Integrated Information Theory, and Predictive Processing theories. A 2025 review and debate article co-authored by proponents of several major theories, including Lamme, describes the present field as one in which there is still more disagreement than agreement, including on what consciousness is, how to identify conscious states, and what a theory of consciousness must explain.

Philosophically, Lamme is close to first-order theories of consciousness. A conscious visual state does not need a higher-order representation of itself in order to be conscious. It need not be available to report. It need not be globally broadcast. It need not be linguistically accessible. It becomes conscious when the first-order perceptual representation itself enters the right sort of recurrent organization. Stanford’s overview of the neuroscience of consciousness describes Lamme’s view as holding that recurrent processing in sensory areas is necessary and sufficient for perceptual consciousness, while entry into the global workspace is not necessary. It also characterizes the view as an overflow theory, because phenomenal consciousness can exist without global access.

In relation to older philosophy of mind, Lamme rejects several classical assumptions at once. Against Cartesian-style privileged access, he denies that introspection or report should be the final authority in drawing the conscious/unconscious boundary. Against behaviorism, he denies that externally measurable report or action exhausts consciousness. Against higher-order theories, he denies that a mental state needs a metarepresentation in order to be experienced. Against global-workspace functionalism, he denies that being globally available for reasoning, memory, decision, and speech is what makes a percept conscious. Yet he is not an eliminativist: he explicitly says he accepts that there is something real and scientifically tractable about conscious experience and that he “embraces rather than denies” qualia, though not in their strictest, nonfunctional form.

The result is a distinctive historical position: Lamme is a physicalist realist about qualia, but a revisionist about how qualia should be scientifically identified. He takes phenomenal consciousness seriously, but he thinks the folk-psychological and report-based concept of consciousness is too contaminated by language, attention, access, and executive function to guide neuroscience reliably. This is why his work matters philosophically: it is not only an empirical theory of visual cortex, but an attempt to change the explanandum of consciousness science itself.

2. The “neural stance”: Lamme’s methodological revolution

Lamme’s methodological starting point is that consciousness has traditionally been defined in mental or psychological terms, with introspective or behavioral observations treated as the “gold standard” against which neural data are fitted. He argues that this creates deep problems for the mind–brain relation. Neural and behavioral measures, he says, should instead be placed on an equal footing; only by allowing neuroscience to reshape our concept of consciousness can progress be made.

His split-brain example is central. Split-brain patients may verbally deny seeing objects shown in the left visual field, because those objects are processed by the right hemisphere, which lacks speech access. Yet some patients can draw, select, match, or otherwise act on those “unseen” objects with the left hand. Are they seeing them? Lamme’s point is that behavior alone does not decide the issue. Whether one counts speech, drawing, matching, or action as evidence of consciousness depends on prior assumptions about language and consciousness.

This motivates his move beyond ordinary “neural correlates of consciousness.” He wants neuroscience not merely to correlate brain activity with pre-given introspective categories, but to participate in defining the categories. Consciousness should not be inferred only from behavior, with neural measurements treated as subordinate. Instead, behavioral data, neural data, introspection, and theory should be jointly used to construct better scientific concepts.

Lamme later clarifies that this is not “neuroscience chauvinism.” A proper science of consciousness, he says, should combine evidence from introspection, behavior, neuroscience, and theory. His view starts from basic introspective anchors: there are clear cases where conscious sensation is present and clear cases where it is absent. But at the ambiguous boundaries—masking, neglect, split brain, change blindness, inattentional blindness, attentional blink—introspection and behavior cannot simply dictate the answer. Neuroscience must be one participant in drawing the boundary, and introspection does not get to set the agenda merely because it came first.

This is the deepest philosophical move in Lamme’s work. He is not just proposing a mechanism; he is proposing conceptual engineering for consciousness science. The concept of consciousness should be carved at the joints of the brain’s causal architecture, not at the joints of our inherited folk vocabulary.

3. Feedforward processing, recurrent processing, and the basic neural thesis

Lamme’s core neural distinction is between feedforward processing and recurrent processing.

Feedforward processing is the rapid sweep of activity from retina through successive visual areas and onward into higher-level, motor, and frontal systems. It can extract features, categorize objects, activate responses, and even support surprisingly complex behavior. Lamme emphasizes that the visual system can perform much of its intelligent work through this feedforward sweep. In his 2018 account, he describes the feedforward sweep as a sophisticated reflex arc: sensory input is swiftly converted into motor possibilities, sometimes including decision-like behavior, before one consciously knows what has happened.

But for Lamme, feedforward processing is not enough for consciousness. The feedforward sweep can activate visual, motor, and even prefrontal regions while the subject remains unaware of the stimulus. Backward masking is a paradigmatic case: a briefly presented target followed by a mask can become invisible, while information about it still travels widely through the brain. Lamme’s conclusion is that the feedforward sweep, however intelligent, does not produce conscious visual sensation.

Recurrent or re-entrant processing begins when neural populations that initially responded separately begin to influence one another through horizontal connections within an area and feedback connections between higher and lower areas. In vision, this means that V1 and other visual areas do not merely pass information forward; they enter dynamic loops in which context, grouping, figure-ground structure, depth, occlusion, and perceptual organization emerge.

Lamme’s early “linking proposition” already stated the thesis clearly: feedforward activation of cells in any brain area is not sufficient for visual awareness; recurrent processing mediated by horizontal and feedback connections is necessary. The same paper also insists that phenomenal awareness must be dissociated from visual attention and decision processes.

The mature thesis is stronger than mere necessity. Lamme argues that, at least for visual consciousness, recurrent processing is the best candidate for what makes the difference between unconscious and conscious perception. Prefrontal activation, reportability, and global access explain something else: what one can do with the experience, not what makes the experience occur.

4. The four-stage model of visual processing

Lamme’s most influential architecture divides visual processing into four stages. The key distinction is not simply low-level versus high-level, nor sensory cortex versus frontal cortex. It is whether processing is feedforward or recurrent, and whether recurrence remains local or becomes widespread.

Stage Neural character Psychological status in Lamme’s reconstruction
Stage 1 Superficial feedforward processing in visual areas Unconscious feature processing
Stage 2 Deep feedforward processing reaching higher, motor, or prefrontal areas Unconscious but behaviorally potent processing
Stage 3 Local or superficial recurrent processing, especially within visual areas Phenomenal visual consciousness without direct access/report
Stage 4 Widespread recurrent processing involving sensory, frontoparietal, motor, executive, and report systems Access consciousness, reportability, working memory

Lamme’s own description is that many objects in a scene evoke Stage 3 recurrent processing limited to visual areas, while only some evoke Stage 4 widespread recurrence resembling global workspace activation. When the stimulus disappears, Stage 3 becomes the basis of large-capacity iconic memory, while Stage 4 becomes the basis of limited-capacity working memory.

This yields one of Lamme’s most important claims: attention determines the depth and extent of processing, but recurrence determines phenomenal consciousness. Attention can help a representation become reportable by promoting widespread recurrent processing. But attention is not what makes a representation phenomenally conscious in the first place. In his 2003 and 2004 work, Lamme explicitly argues that attention and visual awareness must be separated, and that attention determines whether a conscious report is possible, not whether the sensory experience exists.

The four-stage model also sharply distinguishes Lamme from Global Neuronal Workspace Theory. For GNW, Stage 4 is the crucial conscious stage: a representation becomes conscious when it is globally available to multiple systems. For Lamme, Stage 4 explains access, report, and working memory, but Stage 3 is already phenomenally conscious. Stanford’s overview puts the contrast exactly this way: GNW requires Stage 4; Lamme holds that Stage 3 is sufficient for consciousness.

5. Seeing versus knowing that one sees

The heart of Lamme’s philosophy of mind is the distinction between seeing and knowing that one sees.

For Lamme, Stage 4 conscious contents are reportable because visual activity has become linked to other sensory modalities, motor programs, executive control, and report mechanisms. Stage 3 recurrent processing is limited to visual areas and therefore cannot directly support report. But the absence of report does not imply the absence of visual phenomenality. Lamme says there is no reason to assume that removing the modules enabling access and report also removes the visual sensation.

This gives rise to his “rich vision” thesis. In change blindness, he argues, the problem is not that one did not see the pre-change scene; rather, one rich conscious visual sensation is overwritten by another before it is stored in working memory. In inattentional blindness, one may fail to remember or report the object, but that does not prove there was no conscious visual sensation when it appeared. He provocatively describes such cases as “seeing without knowing.”

This is why Lamme is philosophically aligned with the phenomenal/access distinction associated with Ned Block, though Lamme gives it a neural implementation. Phenomenal consciousness is the “what it is like” of seeing; access consciousness is the availability of that content for report, reasoning, decision, and control. Lamme’s theory says the former can exist without the latter.

The implication is radical: much of what ordinary psychology calls “unconscious” may, in Lamme’s framework, be phenomenally conscious but inaccessible. Conversely, much of what ordinary psychology treats as cognitively sophisticated—categorization, action priming, inhibition, conflict monitoring, even some prefrontal activation—may be unconscious if it remains feedforward. This reverses the usual association between “high-level cognition” and consciousness.

6. Why recurrence should generate phenomenality: perceptual organization

Lamme does not treat recurrence as a magical neural signature. He argues that recurrent processing has the right explanatory profile because it performs the kind of work that conscious visual experience seems to involve: perceptual organization.

Visual consciousness is not just the detection of isolated features. It is seeing a structured world: surfaces, objects, depth relations, occlusion, figure-ground relations, color constancy, grouping, segmentation, and meaningful spatial layout. Lamme argues that many of these computational problems are better solved by recurrent architectures than by purely feedforward architectures. Recurrent processing supports feature grouping, segregation, figure-ground assignment, depth sorting, surfaces, and occlusion—the very operations that transform raw feature detection into visual appearance.

In his reply to commentators, Lamme makes the connection explicit: what he takes from introspection is that qualia are better explained by perceptual organization than by access. He links qualia to the brain’s interpretation of the world, not to the later ability to report that interpretation. Color constancy is a favored example: photoreceptors register different wavelengths under different lighting conditions, but the brain organizes the percept as the same stable color. Conscious vision is therefore not raw registration but organized interpretation.

This places Lamme in a lineage that goes back to Gestalt psychology, but with a neural mechanism. Gestalt theorists emphasized that conscious perception is organized, structured, and whole-like rather than a mere aggregation of sensations. Lamme’s contribution is to identify the neural machinery of such organization with recurrent interactions, especially horizontal and feedback processes in visual cortex.

His theory is therefore not simply “consciousness = recurrence.” A more accurate formulation is: visual phenomenality arises when sensory representations are reorganized by recurrent interactions into integrated perceptual structures.

7. STERP, SUPER, and LEARN: Lamme’s expanded account

In “The Crack of Dawn,” Lamme refines the theory. He argues that consciousness has little to do with the apparent complexity or high-levelness of a visual function. Faces, words, objects, and complex categories can be processed rapidly during the feedforward sweep. What matters is whether a visual operation requires spatiotemporally extended neural processing, involving recurrent interactions across space and time. He calls this the STERP property: conscious representations depend on spatiotemporally extended neural processing mediated by recurrent interactions.

He then adds two further properties. The SUPER property concerns the building of superposed or integrated representations that individual modules cannot provide on their own. The LEARN property concerns synaptic plasticity and learning: recurrent interactions operate along plastic pathways and may help build new visual functions that, once learned, can later run automatically and unconsciously.

This gives Lamme a functional story about consciousness. Consciousness is not primarily for verbal report. It is not primarily for rational deliberation. It is not primarily for global broadcasting. Its function is closer to constructive perceptual reorganization and learning: it allows the brain to build new integrated perceptual structures through extended recurrent interaction, after which some of those functions may become routinized and no longer require conscious processing. Lamme is careful that the link between consciousness and learning remains controversial, but he treats it as a promising route to a molecular and functional account of consciousness.

This is one of the strongest places where a full philosophy of mind can be extracted. Lamme’s mind is not a theater, not a language faculty, not a workspace alone, and not a Cartesian inner witness. It is a set of dynamic biological processes by which the brain organizes, stabilizes, and learns structured representations of the world.

8. Ontology: what kind of thing is consciousness?

Lamme’s ontology is physicalist, but not eliminativist. He does not deny qualia; he naturalizes them. Qualia are not irreducible private objects floating above neural activity, but neither are they illusions to be explained away. They are real features of certain neural processes, especially those that organize perception through recurrent interaction.

This makes his view a kind of neural identity theory, but not a crude one. He is not saying that every mental kind maps neatly onto one brain area. He is saying that the conscious/unconscious distinction should be identified with a type of neural causal organization. The relevant identity is not “consciousness = V1” or “consciousness = frontal cortex,” but rather “visual phenomenality = appropriately organized recurrent perceptual processing.”

This ontology is also revisionary. Lamme thinks cognitive psychology often treats mental constructs as undeniable truths to which neuroscience must be fitted. He rejects that. There are no undeniable truths in the study of consciousness, including intuitions about what must be reportable, accessible, attended, or introspectively known. Scientific progress may require setting aside intuitions about consciousness in the same way other sciences have overturned intuitive appearances.

Yet the revision is not total. Lamme starts from the intuition that conscious experience exists and that deep masking or dreamless sleep lack visual sensation, while ordinary seeing with detailed report has it. His radicalism concerns the intermediate cases, where ordinary report-based criteria become unstable. In those cases, he wants neural theory to help draw the boundary.

The resulting metaphysics could be called recurrent realist physicalism: consciousness is real, qualitative, and phenomenal, but it is identical with, or at least fully constituted by, certain recurrent neural processes of perceptual organization.

9. Epistemology: how can consciousness be known scientifically?

Lamme’s epistemology is anti-foundationalist about consciousness. Neither first-person report, nor third-person behavior, nor neural measurement alone is an infallible foundation. Each is evidence; none is sovereign.

This is why Lamme’s work naturally supports no-report paradigms. In a 2015 paper co-authored with Tsuchiya, Wilke, and Frässle, the authors argue that requiring reports has biased the search for neural correlates of consciousness. The goal of no-report paradigms is to dissociate neural activity that gives rise to consciousness from neural activity that enables report.

This matters because report is itself a cognitive act. It requires attention, memory, decision, motor preparation, language or response mapping, and often prefrontal/executive activity. If one identifies the neural correlate of consciousness by comparing “reported seen” versus “reported unseen,” one risks mistaking the machinery of report for the machinery of seeing. Lamme’s whole theory is designed to avoid that confound.

The epistemological cost is serious. If Stage 3 consciousness is not directly reportable, how can we know it is conscious? Lamme’s answer is inference to the best explanation: Stage 3 shares the neural ingredient that best explains Stage 4 phenomenality—recurrent perceptual organization—while lacking only the access machinery. Therefore, we should attribute phenomenality to Stage 3. Critics respond that this is difficult to test because the very absence of access blocks direct confirmation. Stanford’s discussion notes this exact challenge: Stage 3 is not introspectively reportable, so sufficiency is hard to establish independently.

The epistemological structure of Lamme’s theory is therefore abductive rather than demonstrative. It does not “prove” Stage 3 phenomenality by report. It argues that attributing phenomenality to Stage 3 yields a cleaner ontology, a better distinction between seeing and access, and a more coherent explanation of visual neuroscience.

10. Consciousness, attention, memory, and access

Lamme’s theory sharply separates four notions often conflated:

Attention is selective deepening or prioritization of processing. It increases the chance that a representation will be processed deeply, stored, acted on, and reported. But attention does not define consciousness.

Phenomenal consciousness is the presence of perceptual experience. It arises with recurrent perceptual organization.

Access consciousness is the availability of a content for report, working memory, decision, and controlled action. It requires wider recurrent integration involving frontoparietal, motor, and executive systems.

Working memory is not the same as consciousness. Working memory is a limited-capacity, stable representation associated with Stage 4. Iconic or fragile visual memory reflects traces of richer Stage 3 processing but is not itself the original visual phenomenality; it is evidence of what was phenomenally represented moments earlier.

This allows Lamme to reinterpret classic phenomena. Blindsight is not simply unconscious vision, but a case where visual processing may support action without normal recurrent visual organization. Change blindness is not literal blindness but failure to preserve or compare rich visual experience in working memory. Inattentional blindness may be inattentional amnesia or failure of access. Split-brain cases may involve phenomenality in a hemisphere that cannot speak. Neglect and extinction may be access/report pathologies rather than absence of all visual experience.

The philosophical upshot is that the mind is layered. Intelligent processing, attention, consciousness, access, memory, and report can dissociate. Consciousness is not the top of a cognitive pyramid; it is a mode of perceptual organization that can exist beneath, beside, or prior to reflective cognition.

11. Relation to Global Workspace, Higher-Order Theory, and IIT

Lamme’s relation to Global Neuronal Workspace Theory is partly adversarial and partly conciliatory. He accepts that widespread recurrent processing explains access, reportability, and working memory. But he denies that this explains seeing itself. In his phrase, GNW is excellent for explaining access, not seeing.

Against Higher-Order Theories, Lamme denies that a perceptual state becomes conscious only when represented by a higher-order state. Higher-order theories typically tie consciousness to metacognitive or prefrontal mechanisms, whereas Lamme locates visual phenomenality in first-order recurrent sensory processing. Stanford’s overview contrasts these directly: for HOT, higher-order states tied to prefrontal areas are necessary and sufficient; for RPT, recurrent sensory processing is necessary and sufficient for perceptual consciousness.

His relation to Integrated Information Theory is more complex. Lamme is sympathetic to the idea that consciousness involves integration and interdependence, and he uses IIT-like reasoning to argue that recurrent processing has higher integrated structure than feedforward processing. But he also worries that theories based only on integration, recurrence, or information risk panpsychism unless they identify additional “missing ingredients.” In 2018 he argues that many theories may seem to grant consciousness too widely, including to animals, living beings, devices, or even lifeless objects unless lower bounds are specified.

This yields a moderated position. Lamme does not want consciousness to be restricted to adult human report. But he also does not want every recurrent circuit, thermostat, or weakly integrated information processor to be conscious. The problem is to specify what kind of recurrence, integration, spatial-temporal extension, perceptual organization, biological plasticity, or functional sophistication marks the transition.

12. Panpsychism, animals, and disorders of consciousness

Lamme’s theory pushes toward a wider distribution of consciousness than report-based theories allow. If phenomenal consciousness does not require language, report, or human-style reflection, then many animals may have conscious states. Split-brain right hemispheres, infants, neglected visual fields, and some patients with disorders of consciousness become live candidates for consciousness if the relevant recurrent organization is present.

In 2018, Lamme explicitly addresses the panpsychism problem. He notes that recurrent processing may not grind to a complete halt under anesthesia and that some recurrence can remain in states we would still call unconscious. This means mere recurrence is probably not enough. Some additional ingredient is needed: perhaps meaningful information integration, the right spatial and temporal scale, recurrent interactions that change network dynamics, or molecular/plasticity mechanisms such as NMDA-related processes.

He also suggests that consciousness may be graded in richness and quality across species and states. It may make sense to say a fly has conscious/unconscious state transitions, while also saying fly consciousness is vastly poorer than human waking consciousness. Denying all such transitions to nonhuman animals would be human-chauvinistic and would undermine consciousness as a real biological phenomenon distinct from human cognitive sophistication.

The philosophical stance is therefore neither strict human exceptionalism nor flat panpsychism. It is a biological pluralism of consciousness: many systems may have consciousness if they instantiate the right recurrent-organizational processes, but the richness, structure, and moral significance of those conscious states vary enormously.

13. Artificial consciousness: what Lamme implies, and what he does not fully develop

Lamme does mention machines and devices, but he does not, in the sources examined here, offer a full theory of artificial consciousness comparable to his theory of visual consciousness. His 2018 paper asks whether theories need further ingredients that would place a lower bound on the beings or devices that have conscious experience. That is a direct opening toward artificial consciousness, but the discussion is mainly about the boundary problem and panpsychism, not about contemporary AI architectures.

A Lammean approach to artificial consciousness would reject any simple behavioral test. A system’s ability to say “I am conscious,” classify images, answer questions, or imitate human reports would not be decisive. Report is exactly what Lamme thinks can mislead us. The relevant question would be whether the system has internal causal dynamics functionally analogous to recurrent perceptual organization: input modules with algorithmic recurrence, organized integrated perceptual representations, spatiotemporally extended interactions, and perhaps plasticity-like mechanisms that support learning of new representational structures.

This extrapolation is consistent with recent AI-consciousness work, though it is not Lamme’s own detailed theory. Butlin and colleagues’ 2023 report on consciousness in AI derives theory-based “indicator properties” from several consciousness theories, including Recurrent Processing Theory. For RPT, the listed indicators include input modules using algorithmic recurrence and input modules generating organized, integrated perceptual representations. The report’s own analysis was that no then-current AI systems were conscious, while also finding no obvious technical barrier to building AI systems satisfying the indicators.

The hardest issue is whether Lamme’s theory is substrate-specific. Much of his evidence concerns cortex, horizontal and feedback connections, NMDA receptors, GABA, synaptic plasticity, anesthesia, and biological perceptual learning. That biological emphasis may suggest that mere computational recurrence is not sufficient. On the other hand, his methodological stance—defining consciousness by explanatory causal properties—could be generalized to nonbiological systems if they instantiate the relevant organization. Lamme himself does not settle this in a systematic AI theory, so the safest reconstruction is: artificial consciousness is possible in principle under a Lammean framework only if artificial systems reproduce the relevant recurrent, integrated, perceptual-organizational dynamics, not merely the outward behavior or report of consciousness.

14. The implied philosophy of mind, distilled

The philosophy of mind that can be extracted from Lamme can be stated as a set of theses.

First, phenomenal consciousness is a real natural phenomenon. It is not reducible to verbal report, public behavior, or introspective certainty. Lamme accepts qualia, but in a softened, scientifically tractable form tied to perceptual organization rather than ineffable nonfunctional properties.

Second, the conscious/unconscious distinction should be drawn neurally as well as behaviorally. Folk concepts and introspective intuitions are useful at clear extremes but unreliable at the margins. Neuroscience can legitimately revise the concept of consciousness.

Third, visual consciousness is first-order recurrent perceptual organization. A visual state becomes phenomenally conscious when sensory representations enter recurrent interactions that integrate, organize, and structure perceptual content.

Fourth, access is not phenomenality. Global availability, report, working memory, language, and executive use are downstream or additional functions. They explain what one can do with an experience, not what makes it an experience.

Fifth, attention is not consciousness. Attention affects which representations become deep, stable, reportable, and remembered. But unattended or inaccessible representations may still be phenomenally conscious if they undergo the right recurrent organization.

Sixth, unconscious processing can be intelligent. Feedforward processing can categorize, guide action, activate prefrontal regions, trigger inhibition, and support forms of cognitive control without consciousness. The line between conscious and unconscious is therefore not the line between simple and complex, low-level and high-level, sensory and prefrontal, or passive and active.

Seventh, consciousness is richer than report. What is phenomenally present may exceed what can be stabilized in working memory or expressed in report. The richness of experience is fragile and can be overwritten, forgotten, or rendered inaccessible.

Eighth, the function of consciousness is constructive and plastic. Conscious visual processing helps build integrated perceptual structures and may contribute to learning new visual functions, which can later become automatic.

Ninth, nonhuman and nonverbal consciousness is a serious possibility. If consciousness does not require language or report, then animals, infants, split-brain hemispheres, and some neurologically impaired patients cannot be excluded merely because they lack standard report.

Tenth, artificial consciousness cannot be assessed by behavior alone. A Lammean test would examine internal recurrent perceptual organization, not mere linguistic self-ascription or task performance.

15. Critical assessment

Lamme’s theory has several major strengths. It directly addresses the report confound that haunts consciousness science. It gives a mechanistic account of why masking, blindsight, change blindness, attentional blink, neglect, split-brain phenomena, and no-report paradigms matter. It offers a biologically plausible bridge between phenomenology and visual neuroscience: conscious seeing is not arbitrary neural activation, but organized perceptual structuring through recurrent interaction. It also gives phenomenal consciousness a clearer ontology than theories that identify it with report, since report is plainly a mixture of perception, memory, decision, action, and language.

The central weakness is the sufficiency claim. It is plausible that recurrent processing is necessary for visual consciousness. It is harder to prove that local Stage 3 recurrence is sufficient, because by definition Stage 3 lacks direct access and report. The theory therefore relies on abductive and theoretical arguments: Stage 3 shares the neural feature that best explains Stage 4 phenomenality, while lacking only access mechanisms. Critics can accept Stage 3 recurrence as necessary or correlated while denying that it is sufficient.

A second weakness is the risk of over-attribution. If one says that unattended or unreported visual processing is phenomenally conscious, one may appear to stretch “consciousness” beyond ordinary use. Lamme welcomes this revision, but the cost is conceptual controversy: some will say he has changed the subject from consciousness as ordinarily meant to a neural kind he chooses to call consciousness.

A third weakness is the boundary problem. If recurrence is common, what prevents consciousness from being everywhere? Lamme recognizes this in his 2018 panpsychism discussion and introduces possible missing ingredients: meaningful integration, spatial-temporal extent, plasticity, and richer causal structure. But this also complicates the simple early formula “recurrent processing is sufficient.”

A fourth weakness is generalization. Lamme’s theory is strongest for visual perceptual consciousness. It is less clear how it handles bodily feeling, pain, emotion, self-consciousness, abstract thought, inner speech, temporal experience, dreaming, or affective consciousness. A general Lammean philosophy of mind must either extend the recurrent-organization model to other domains or concede that visual consciousness is only one species of consciousness.

The fifth weakness concerns artificial consciousness. Lamme’s theory gives a principled reason to distrust purely behavioral AI tests, but it does not fully specify whether biological mechanisms such as NMDA-dependent plasticity are essential or merely one implementation of a more abstract recurrent-organizational structure. That ambiguity matters greatly for AI.

Conclusion

Victor Lamme’s contribution is historically important because he pushes consciousness science from the search for neural correlates of reportable experience toward a more radical project: letting neuroscience reshape the concept of consciousness itself. His theory occupies the local-recurrent, first-order, overflow side of the modern debate. It is opposed to identifying consciousness with global workspace access, higher-order thought, attention, working memory, or report, while accepting that those mechanisms explain important cognitive consequences of consciousness.

The philosophy of mind extracted from Lamme is a form of recurrent phenomenal realism. Consciousness is real, qualitative, and natural. It is not private in the Cartesian sense, not behavioral in the behaviorist sense, not metacognitive in the higher-order sense, and not globally broadcast in the workspace sense. At least in vision, it is the brain’s recurrent organization of perceptual content into structured appearance. Access lets us know and say what we see; recurrent perceptual organization is what makes there be seeing at all.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
